symbols.name: q_n
symbols.def: Lower-bound weak-arm scale: q_0 if gamma=0 else h_n^{beta_ag}.

symbols.name: M_n
symbols.def: Minimax regret inf_{pi_hat} sup_{P} E_P R_P(pi_hat).

symbols.name: U_n
symbols.def: Feasible upper risk sup_P E_P R_P(pi_hat_n).

symbols.name: B_n
symbols.def: Active block of the two-point witness.

symbols.name: qbar_n
symbols.def: Feasible clip: 1/4 if beta_ag=0 else hbar_n^{beta_ag}.

symbols.name: L_n
symbols.def: Logarithmic design scale (e.g. log n) controlling cell count and per-cell sampling.

assumptions.condition: O_1, ..., O_n are i.i.d. draws from P.
(Y(0), Y(1)) is independent of A given X under P.
P(0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u) <= C_m u^alpha for all 0 < u <= u_0.
Either P_X{tau_P(X)=0}=0, or every pi in Pi agrees with pi_star P_X-almost everywhere on {tau_P(X)=0}.
P{p_P(X) <= v, 0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u} <= C_o u^alpha v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o u^gamma, with v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.

novel.justification: The joint margin/one-sided-overlap envelope couples the Tsybakov margin exponent alpha to the polynomial overlap-decay exponent gamma in a single tail bound. It is the class-defining condition whose induced minimax exponent r_*(alpha,gamma) is the paper's contribution; it strictly generalises the strict-overlap (gamma=0) margin class and the constant-margin overlap-decay class.

assumptions.condition: Pi is a class of measurable deterministic policies with finite VC dimension d_Pi < infinity.
pi_star is an element of Pi for every law P in the class.

For the finite-VC class Pi there is a localized Rademacher/Dudley entropy-integral envelope psi with psi(r) <= C sqrt(d_Pi/n) r polylog(n) controlling the localized offset empirical processes uniformly over the class.

standard.name: localized Rademacher / Dudley entropy-integral envelope (VC class)

assumptions.condition: P{p_P(X) <= v} <= C_om v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o, with the convention v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.

novel.justification: Marginal one-sided polynomial overlap decay: the propensity-tail mass P{p<=v} decays as v^{1/gamma}. The marginal companion of the joint ass:overlap-decay; it controls the clipped-region estimation-drift mass in the FEASIBLE upper bound. The two-point witness satisfies it (rare-block mass h^alpha <= C (h^beta)^{1/gamma}=C h^{alpha/(alpha+1)} for small h), so it does not weaken the converse.

assumptions.condition: The cross-fitted nuisance estimators satisfy ||muhat_a-mu_a||_{L2(P)} <= r_mu_n and ||ehat-e||_{L2(P)} <= r_e_n for a in {0,1}, with product-rate r_mu_n r_e_n = O(n^{-1/2}).
When gamma=0, p_P(X) >= underline_p holds P_X-almost surely, for a constant underline_p in (0,1/2].
The cross-fitted outcome-regression estimates muhat_0, muhat_1 take values in [-1,1].
There exist a >= 0, c >= 1/2 and constants C_mu, C_prod such that r_mu_n <= C_mu n^(-a) and r_mu_n r_e_n <= C_prod n^(-c) for all large n.

For the cross-fitted propensity estimator used in def:feasible-erm and each analysis clip q, with ehat_q(x)=min{1-q,max{q,ehat(x)}}, there is a constant C_v such that, conditionally on the training folds, e_P(x)/ehat_q(x)^2 + (1-e_P(x))/(1-ehat_q(x))^2 <= C_v/max{p_P(x),q} for P_X-almost every x.

novel.justification: standard: overlap-compatible clipped-propensity stability; it follows, for example, from a uniform constant-factor bound ehat_q(x) asymp max{e_P(x),q} and 1-ehat_q(x) asymp max{1-e_P(x),q}, a common stability condition in weak-overlap IPW and AIPW analyses.

definitions.name: R_P
definitions.construction: V_P(pi) = E_P[pi(X) tau_P(X)]; pi_star(x) = 1{tau_P(x) >= 0}; R_P(pi) = V_P(pi_star) - V_P(pi).

definitions.name: (beta_ag, D_ag, r_star)
definitions.construction: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

definitions.name: (s_feas, r_feas)
definitions.construction: Let A_alpha=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha). For certified polynomial nuisance exponents a,c with r_mu_n=O(n^{-a}) and r_mu_n r_e_n=O(n^{-c}), c>=1/2: the clipped-AIPW ERM welfare regret is bounded by the GLOBAL (additive, NON-localized) estimated-nuisance drift r_mu_n r_e_n/q + r_mu_n q^{1/(2 gamma)} plus a localized stochastic term whose radius uses the AIPW conditional VARIANCE Var(Gamma_q|X) <~ 1/max{p,q} (scale n^{-1/2} q^{-1/2}), NOT the crude envelope q^{-1}. Balancing the clip q=n^{-s} gives g(a,c,gamma)=sup_{s>=0} min{A_alpha(1-s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)} and r_feas=min{r_star(alpha,gamma), g(a,c,gamma)}. At gamma=0 (strict-overlap floor, q fixed) r_feas=min{A_alpha, c}, which equals r_star=A_alpha when c>=A_alpha. The drift is GLOBAL because ||b_q||_2 cannot carry the r_mu r_e product from L2 bounds on each factor; only E|b_q| (Cauchy-Schwarz, L1) gives the product, and that enters regret additively, not through the margin self-bound.

definitions.name: e_q
definitions.construction: e_q(x; eta) = min{1-q, max{q, e(x)}} for clip q in (0,1/2] and nuisance eta=(mu_0,mu_1,e).

definitions.name: Gamma_q
definitions.construction: Gamma_q(O; eta) = mu_1(X) - mu_0(X) + (A/e_q(X;eta))(Y - mu_1(X)) - ((1-A)/(1-e_q(X;eta)))(Y - mu_0(X)).

definitions.name: pi_hat_n
definitions.construction: Vhat_{n,q}(pi) = n^{-1} sum_i pi(X_i) Gamma_q(O_i; etahat^{(-k(i))}) with K-fold cross-fitted nuisances etahat and clip q in (0,1/2]; pi_hat_n in argmax_{pi in Pi} Vhat_{n,q}(pi). The clip q is the analysis tuning parameter (chosen by the bias-variance balance).

definitions.name: M_n
definitions.construction: M_n(alpha,gamma) = inf over measurable data-dependent Pi-valued estimators pi_hat of sup_{P in P_class} E_P R_P(pi_hat).

definitions.name: U_n
definitions.construction: U_n(alpha,gamma; etahat) = sup_{P in P_class} E_P R_P(pi_hat_n).

definitions.name: P_class
definitions.construction: { P : ass:iid, ass:consistency, ass:unconfoundedness, ass:bounded-outcome, ass:positivity, ass:margin, ass:zero-effect, ass:overlap-decay, ass:marginal-overlap, ass:strict-overlap-endpoint, ass:policy-class, and ass:optimal-in-class hold at exponents (alpha,gamma) }

definitions.name: (P_{n,+}, P_{n,-})
definitions.construction: On X=[0,1] with P_X=Lebesgue, fix h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, q_n=(1/4 if beta_ag=0 else h_n^{beta_ag}), and an active block B_n=[0, c_B h_n^alpha] with P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^alpha. The two laws P_{n,sigma}, sigma in {+,-}, share P_X, share the logging propensity e(x)=q_n on B_n and e(x)=1/2 off B_n, and share an off-block outcome law with common optimal label 1 and contrast tau_0 > u_0. On B_n set mu_0(x)=0 and let the treated outcome on the active treated cell {X in B_n, A=1} satisfy P(Y=1 | X in B_n, A=1)=(1+sigma h_n)/2, so tau_{P_{n,sigma}}=sigma h_n on B_n. The laws agree everywhere off the treated active cell. Constants are chosen with 8 C_B C_Q < log 5.

statements.statement: Under ass:iid and ass:bounded-outcome, for every deterministic pi in Pi, R_P(pi) = E_P[ |tau_P(X)| 1{pi(X) != pi_star(X)} ] = integral over X of |tau_P(x)| 1{pi(x) != pi_star(x)} dP_X(x).
statements.proof_tex: Fix a deterministic policy pi in Pi. By def:welfare-regret, V_P(pi_star)-V_P(pi)=E_P[(pi_star(X)-pi(X)) tau_P(X)]. The bounded-outcome assumption gives mu_a in [-1,1], hence tau_P in [-2,2], so all displayed expectations are finite. For each x, if tau_P(x)>0 then pi_star(x)=1 and (pi_star(x)-pi(x)) tau_P(x) equals tau_P(x) when pi(x)=0 and equals 0 when pi(x)=1. This is exactly |tau_P(x)| 1{pi(x) != pi_star(x)}. If tau_P(x)<0 then pi_star(x)=0 and (pi_star(x)-pi(x)) tau_P(x) equals -tau_P(x) when pi(x)=1 and equals 0 when pi(x)=0, again equal to |tau_P(x)| 1{pi(x) != pi_star(x)}. If tau_P(x)=0, both sides are zero, regardless of the tie convention in pi_star. Therefore (pi_star-pi) tau_P=|tau_P| 1{pi != pi_star} pointwise P_X-a.s. Taking expectation over X gives R_P(pi)=E_P[|tau_P(X)| 1{pi(X) != pi_star(X)}]=integral |tau_P(x)| 1{pi(x) != pi_star(x)} dP_X(x). The iid sampling assumption is not needed for this population identity.
statements.justification: Regret equals the |contrast|-weighted disagreement mass; the exponent-free identity underlying margin localization and the converse separation.

statements.statement: Under ass:margin and ass:zero-effect there is C=C(C_m,u_0,alpha) such that for every pi in Pi, P_X(D_pi) <= C R_P(pi)^(alpha/(1+alpha)).
statements.route: Corner R_P(pi)=0: then D_pi has P_X-measure 0 (the disagreement contributes zero |tau|-weighted mass via thm:welfare-identity), so the bound P_X(D_pi)<=C R_P(pi)^{alpha/(1+alpha)} holds trivially (both sides 0 when P_X{tau=0 disagreement}=0 by ass:zero-effect); state this case explicitly.
statements.proof_tex: Let D_pi={x: pi(x) != pi_star(x)}. By thm:welfare-identity, R_P(pi)=integral_{D_pi} |tau_P(x)| dP_X(x). First suppose R_P(pi)=0. Then |tau_P| 1_{D_pi}=0 P_X-a.s. Hence D_pi can have positive mass only inside {tau_P=0}. Under ass:zero-effect either P_X{tau_P=0}=0, or every pi in Pi agrees with pi_star P_X-a.s. on {tau_P=0}; in both cases P_X(D_pi)=0, so the claimed inequality holds. Now suppose R=R_P(pi)>0. For any u in (0,u_0], decompose D_pi into the zero-effect set, the small-margin set, and the set {|tau_P|>u}. The zero-effect part has P_X-mass zero by ass:zero-effect. The margin assumption gives P_X(0<|tau_P(X)|<=u)<=C_m u^alpha. On {|tau_P|>u}, the welfare identity gives u P_X(D_pi cap {|tau_P|>u}) <= integral_{D_pi} |tau_P| dP_X=R. Thus P_X(D_pi)<=C_m u^alpha+R/u for every u in (0,u_0]. If R^{1/(1+alpha)}<=u_0, choose u=R^{1/(1+alpha)} and get P_X(D_pi)<=(C_m+1) R^{alpha/(1+alpha)}. If R^{1/(1+alpha)}>u_0, then P_X(D_pi)<=1<=u_0^{-alpha} R^{alpha/(1+alpha)}. Taking C=max{C_m+1,u_0^{-alpha}} proves P_X(D_pi)<=C R_P(pi)^{alpha/(1+alpha)}. For alpha=0 the same display reads P_X(D_pi)<=C and is immediate from the preceding bound with exponent zero.
statements.justification: Tsybakov margin converts regret into disagreement mass at the fast exponent alpha/(1+alpha); supplies the variance localization for the achievability empirical process.

statements.statement: For alpha >= 0, gamma > 0, h in (0,1), beta >= 0, at the tight window v=h^beta, u=h^(beta/gamma): u^alpha v^(1/gamma) = h^((alpha+1)beta/gamma), and u^alpha v^(1/gamma) >= h^alpha iff beta <= alpha gamma/(alpha+1), with equality at beta = beta_ag. Hence a block of mass ~ h^alpha, contrast ~ h, propensity ~ h^beta meets the ass:overlap-decay envelope iff beta <= beta_ag, and beta_ag is the least informative admissible weak-arm exponent.
statements.proof_tex: Let gamma>0, h in (0,1), beta>=0, v=h^beta, and u=h^{beta/gamma}. Then u^alpha v^{1/gamma}=(h^{beta/gamma})^alpha (h^beta)^{1/gamma}=h^{(alpha+1) beta/gamma}. Since h lies in (0,1), h^a >= h^alpha holds if and only if a<=alpha. Applying this with a=(alpha+1) beta/gamma gives u^alpha v^{1/gamma}>=h^alpha if and only if beta<=alpha gamma/(alpha+1). By def:exponents this endpoint is beta_ag, and equality holds when beta=beta_ag. For an active block with mass proportional to h^alpha, contrast h, and propensity h^beta, the least favorable overlap check is the smallest admissible window that both includes the weak-arm propensity layer and satisfies v<=c_o u^gamma. Up to constants this window is v=h^beta and u=h^{beta/gamma}. The overlap envelope permits the block precisely when its mass h^alpha is no larger than the right side u^alpha v^{1/gamma}, which is equivalent to beta<=beta_ag. Larger beta means smaller propensity h^beta and therefore less information; beta_ag is the largest admissible, hence least informative, weak-arm exponent.
statements.justification: Characterizes which weak-arm propensity layer the overlap-decay envelope admits; pins the maximal (least informative) admissible exponent beta_ag that the converse construction uses.

statements.statement: For all large n the corrected witness laws P_{n,+}, P_{n,-} satisfy ass:iid, ass:consistency, ass:unconfoundedness, ass:bounded-outcome, ass:positivity, ass:margin, ass:zero-effect, and ass:overlap-decay. If, in addition, the two explicit witness-optimal policies x -> 1 and x -> 1{x notin B_n} belong to Pi, then P_{n,+}, P_{n,-} lie in def:law-class P_class(alpha,gamma). (Normalization: the margin window satisfies u_0<2, so an off-block contrast tau_0 in (u_0, 2) exists under bounded outcomes |Y|<=1.)
statements.route: Verify each member-property of P_class for the explicit construction. Membership in the overlap-decay envelope is the tight-window equality u^alpha v^(1/gamma)=h^alpha at beta=beta_ag (prop:overlap-envelope); margin tail holds because |tau| in {h_n, tau_0} with the h_n level carrying mass <= C_B h_n^alpha on B_n.
statements.proof_tex: Fix sigma in {+,-}. The witness has X uniform on [0,1], active block B_n of mass m_n=c_B h_n^alpha, propensity e=q_n on B_n and e=1/2 off B_n. For all large n, h_n in (0,u_0) and q_n in (0,1/2], so ass:positivity holds. Choose the off-block contrast tau_0 in (u_0,2), possible because ass:margin-window states u_0<2 and bounded outcomes allow contrasts in [-2,2]. Define potential-outcome conditional distributions equal to the displayed treatment-arm outcome laws and independent of A conditional on X; then consistency and unconfoundedness hold by construction. The outcomes take values in [-1,1], so ass:bounded-outcome holds. The contrast is sigma h_n on B_n and tau_0 off B_n, hence it is never zero; ass:zero-effect holds. For the margin condition, if 0<u<h_n the event {0<|tau|<=u} is empty, while if h_n<=u<=u_0 the event is exactly B_n and has mass c_B h_n^alpha<=c_B u^alpha. Choosing c_B<=C_m gives ass:margin. For the joint overlap-decay condition when gamma>0, off B_n is excluded because tau_0>u_0. If u<h_n or v<q_n the event is empty. Otherwise the event is B_n, but the constraint v<=c_o u^gamma and v>=q_n force u>=(q_n/c_o)^{1/gamma}. Thus u^alpha v^{1/gamma}>=c_o^{-alpha/gamma} q_n^{(alpha+1)/gamma}. At beta_ag=alpha gamma/(alpha+1), q_n=h_n^{beta_ag} when beta_ag>0, and q_n^{(alpha+1)/gamma}=h_n^alpha. Taking c_B small enough relative to C_o and c_o yields c_B h_n^alpha<=C_o u^alpha v^{1/gamma}. If beta_ag=0 the active propensity is the fixed constant 1/4; for v<1/4 the event is empty and for v>=1/4 the same margin bound c_B h_n^alpha<=C_o u^alpha applies after enlarging constants. For gamma=0, the convention v^{1/gamma}=1 gives the same verification: the event is empty unless u>=h_n and v>=q_n=1/4, and then its mass c_B h_n^alpha is at most C_o u^alpha. The enlarged law class also requires marginal overlap. For gamma>0, P{p_P(X)<=v}=0 when v<q_n, is m_n when q_n<=v<1/2, and is at most 1 for v>=1/2. At v=q_n the required right side is C_om q_n^{1/gamma}; because q_n=h_n^{beta_ag} gives q_n^{1/gamma}=h_n^{alpha/(alpha+1)} and h_n^alpha<=h_n^{alpha/(alpha+1)} for alpha>=0 up to the constant case alpha=0, a sufficiently large C_om verifies the bound; larger v only increases the right side, and v>=1/2 is handled by increasing C_om. For gamma=0, strict overlap holds with underline_p=1/4 since p_P is 1/4 on B_n and 1/2 off B_n, and the marginal-overlap convention is trivial after enlarging C_om. The policy pi_star is x -> 1 under P_{n,+} and x -> 1{x notin B_n} under P_{n,-}. If these two policies belong to Pi, then ass:optimal-in-class holds for the witnesses; ass:policy-class is a standing property of Pi. Therefore, for all large n, the two witnesses satisfy every member condition in def:law-class P_class(alpha,gamma).
statements.justification: Replaces the assumed existence of the converse construction (old ass:primitive-cube): the witnesses are CONSTRUCTED and their class membership is DERIVED, not assumed.

statements.statement: For the def:two-point-witness laws, the per-observation chi-square is chi^2(P_{n,+} || P_{n,-}) <= C m_n q_n h_n^2 with m_n=P_X(B_n)~h_n^alpha and q_n~h_n^{beta_ag}, hence ~ h_n^{2+alpha+beta_ag}; for h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)} the n-fold divergence is bounded, chi^2(P_{n,+}^{⊗n} || P_{n,-}^{⊗n}) <= C (equivalently 1+chi^2(P^{⊗n}) = (1+O(h_n^{2+alpha+beta_ag}))^n <= e^{C}).
statements.route: The two laws differ only on the treated active cell {X in B_n, A=1} of mass m_n q_n, where the outcome Bernoulli means are (1 ± h_n)/2; the two-point Bernoulli chi-square is ~ h_n^2 (finite_one_add_chiSqDiv). Restrict to the charged cell and tensorize (one_add_chiSqDiv_pi_iid). Balance n h_n^(2+alpha+beta_ag) ~ 1.
statements.proof_tex: The two witness laws have the same X marginal, the same propensity, and the same conditional outcome law except on the cell C_n={X in B_n,A=1}. The P_{n,-}-probability of C_n is m_n q_n, where m_n=P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^alpha. Conditional on C_n, the outcome is Bernoulli with success probabilities p_+=(1+h_n)/2 and p_-=(1-h_n)/2 under the plus and minus laws. For h_n<=1/2, the Bernoulli chi-square is (p_+-p_-)^2/p_- + ((1-p_+)-(1-p_-))^2/(1-p_-) = h_n^2((1-p_-)^{-1}+p_-^{-1}) <= 8 h_n^2. Since the laws agree off C_n, the full one-observation chi-square is m_n q_n times this conditional chi-square, hence chi^2(P_{n,+}||P_{n,-})<=C m_n q_n h_n^2. By the definitions m_n is proportional to h_n^alpha and q_n is either a fixed constant when beta_ag=0 or h_n^{beta_ag}; in both cases this is bounded by C h_n^{2+alpha+beta_ag}. With h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, the one-observation chi-square is at most C/n. Product chi-square tensorizes as 1+chi^2(P_+^{n}||P_-^{n})=(1+chi^2(P_+||P_-))^n. Therefore 1+chi^2(P_{n,+}^{n}||P_{n,-}^{n}) <= (1+C/n)^n <= exp(C), so the n-fold chi-square is bounded by a constant independent of n.
statements.justification: The information-cost half of the Le Cam two-point bound, derived from the explicit construction.

statements.statement: For the def:two-point-witness laws, inf_{pi in Pi} max_{sigma in {+,-}} R_{P_{n,sigma}}(pi) >= c h_n^(1+alpha): the optimal policies are opposite on B_n, so any pi misclassifies B_n under at least one law and pays regret >= (1/2) P_X(B_n) h_n ~ h_n^(1+alpha).
statements.route: By thm:welfare-identity, regret on B_n equals |tau| times disagreement mass. Since tau_{P_{n,+}}=+h_n and tau_{P_{n,-}}=-h_n on B_n, pi_star flips sign across the two laws on B_n; any fixed pi disagrees with one of them on all of B_n, giving regret >= h_n P_X(B_n)/... ~ h_n^(1+alpha).
statements.proof_tex: On B_n the plus witness has tau=+h_n, so its optimal policy is 1; the minus witness has tau=-h_n, so its optimal policy is 0. Off B_n both witnesses have the same positive contrast tau_0 and the same optimal label 1. By thm:welfare-identity, for any deterministic policy pi, R_{P_{n,+}}(pi) contributes h_n P_X(B_n cap {pi=0}) on B_n, while R_{P_{n,-}}(pi) contributes h_n P_X(B_n cap {pi=1}) on B_n. These two subsets partition B_n because pi is binary. Hence R_{P_{n,+}}(pi)+R_{P_{n,-}}(pi) >= h_n P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^{1+alpha}. Consequently max_{sigma in {+,-}} R_{P_{n,sigma}}(pi) >= (c_B/2) h_n^{1+alpha}. Taking the infimum over pi in Pi gives the claimed separation.
statements.justification: The separation half of the Le Cam two-point bound, derived from the construction.

statements.statement: Le Cam two-point testing bound. For product laws P_+^{\otimes n}, P_-^{\otimes n} with per-observation chi-square chi^2(P_+ || P_-) <= c_0/n, every test psi satisfies inf_psi [P_+^{\otimes n}(psi != +) + P_-^{\otimes n}(psi != -)] >= c_1 > 0 for a constant c_1=c_1(c_0); combined with a parameter separation this yields the two-point minimax lower bound.
statements.proof_tex: This is the classical two-point testing inequality. Tsybakov, Introduction to Nonparametric Estimation, 2009, Chapter 2, Theorems 2.2 and 2.6, gives Le Cam's method and the Bretagnolle-Huber bound: for any test psi between product laws P_+^n and P_-^n, P_+^n(psi != +)+P_-^n(psi != -) >= (1/2) exp(-KL(P_+^n,P_-^n)). Since KL(P_+^n,P_-^n)<=log(1+chi^2(P_+^n||P_-^n)) and 1+chi^2(P_+^n||P_-^n)=(1+chi^2(P_+||P_-))^n, the assumption chi^2(P_+||P_-)<=c_0/n implies KL(P_+^n,P_-^n)<=c_0. Thus every test has summed error at least c_1=(1/2) exp(-c_0). The standard loss-separation form of Le Cam's method in the same references then yields a minimax lower bound equal to the separation radius times this positive testing-error constant, up to the harmless factor used to pass from summed to maximal risk.

statements.statement: Assume the two explicit witness-optimal policies of the corrected def:two-point-witness, namely x -> 1 and x -> 1{x notin B_n}, belong to Pi for all large n. Then M_n(alpha,gamma) = inf_{pi_hat} sup_{P in P_class} E_P R_P(pi_hat) >= c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} for all large n, with c > 0 independent of n.
statements.route: Le Cam two-point reduction: the two in-class witnesses (lem:witness-membership) are statistically near-indistinguishable (bounded n-fold divergence, lem:two-point-divergence) yet force opposite labels with regret separation ~ h_n^(1+alpha) (lem:regret-separation). two_point_lower_bound_of_chiSqDiv_le converts the bounded divergence into a constant testing-error floor, yielding M_n >= c h_n^(1+alpha) = c n^{-r_star}.
statements.proof_tex: By lem:witness-membership, under the stated richness assumption on Pi the two laws P_{n,+} and P_{n,-} are elements of P_class(alpha,gamma) for all large n. Let delta_n=(c_B/2) h_n^{1+alpha}, the separation from lem:regret-separation. Fix any measurable Pi-valued estimator hat pi. From its output define a test psi by psi=+ if R_{P_{n,+}}(hat pi)<=R_{P_{n,-}}(hat pi), and psi=- otherwise. For every deterministic output pi, if psi(pi)=- then R_{P_{n,+}}(pi)>delta_n, because R_{P_{n,+}}(pi)+R_{P_{n,-}}(pi)>=2 delta_n; similarly, if psi(pi)=+ then R_{P_{n,-}}(pi)>=delta_n. Therefore E_{P_{n,+}} R_{P_{n,+}}(hat pi)+E_{P_{n,-}} R_{P_{n,-}}(hat pi) >= delta_n [P_{n,+}^n(psi=-)+P_{n,-}^n(psi=+)]. Lem:two-point-divergence gives chi^2(P_{n,+}||P_{n,-})<=c_0/n for a constant c_0, and lem:le-cam-two-point-chisq gives the bracketed testing error at least c_1>0. Hence max_sigma E_{P_{n,sigma}} R_{P_{n,sigma}}(hat pi) >= (c_1/2) delta_n. Because the witnesses lie in P_class, sup_{P in P_class} E_P R_P(hat pi) is at least this quantity. Taking the infimum over all estimators yields M_n(alpha,gamma)>=c h_n^{1+alpha}. Finally, h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, so h_n^{1+alpha}=n^{-(1+alpha)/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}=n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} by def:exponents.
statements.justification: The novel sharp converse: the margin/one-sided-overlap minimax regret floor, with the least-favorable family CONSTRUCTED from primitives (no assumed cube).
statements.gap: AtheyWager2021 give the n^{-1/2} efficient rate under strict overlap; KitagawaTetenov2018 the VC achievability; Audibert-Tsybakov2007 the margin converse without overlap decay. None give the coupled margin x one-sided-overlap-decay regret floor r_*(alpha,gamma).

statements.statement: CONSTRUCT-AND-DETERMINE. Assume gamma<=alpha+1, ass:marginal-overlap, ass:nuisance-rate, ass:vc-localized-envelope, and at gamma=0 also ass:strict-overlap-endpoint. For the plain cross-fit clipped-AIPW ERM of def:feasible-erm with clip q chosen by the analysis, DERIVE (do NOT pre-specify) the best worst-case welfare-regret exponent r_feas with sup_{P in P_class(alpha,gamma)} E_P R_P(pi_hat_n) <= C n^{-r_feas} (log n)^p, by balancing the localized variance against the estimated-nuisance CLIP BIAS over q. State the clip q and the resulting r_feas in closed form, confirm r_feas<=r_star, and reduce at gamma=0.
statements.route: Use lem:clip-bias's EXACT identity (no assumed cancellation). Bound the policy-weighted drift: |E[(pi-pi_star) b_q]| <= ||ebar_q-e_P||_2 ||Delta/ebar_q||_2. Split ||ebar_q-e_P||_2 <= ||ehat-e||_2 (=r_e_n, off-clip DR product) + ||ehat-ebar_q||_2 (clip displacement, supported on {p<=q}, <= C q * P{p<=q}^{1/2} <= C q^{1+1/(2gamma)} by ass:marginal-overlap); and ||Delta/ebar_q||_2 <= r_mu_n/q (clip floor). So clip bias B <= r_mu_n r_e_n / q + r_mu_n q^{1/(2gamma)}. Variance A = (q^{-1} rho^{alpha/(1+alpha)}/n)^{1/2} (ass:vc-localized-envelope + thm:margin-localization). Self-bound R <= C(A+B)^{(2+2alpha)/(2+alpha)}; MINIMIZE over q; DERIVE the closed-form r_feas(alpha,gamma,r_mu_n,r_e_n) (your output, proof must match) and reduce at gamma=0. The 1/q-amplified DR product is the weak-overlap (Dorn) effect; keep it, do not cancel it.
statements.proof_tex: The construction is the cross-fitted clipped-AIPW ERM in def:feasible-erm with analysis clip q_n=n^{-s_n} for gamma>0, where s_n is chosen to maximize the balance phi(s)=min{A_alpha(1-s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)} up to an immaterial logarithmic grid error. At gamma=0 choose any fixed q<=underline_p. The exact drift identity is lem:clip-bias. Lem:clipped-score-drift-bound converts it into the honest global additive drift B_q<=C(r_mu,n r_e,n/q+r_mu,n q^{1/(2 gamma)}) for gamma>0, and B_q<=C r_mu,n r_e,n under strict overlap. Lem:clipped-score-variance-envelope gives the localized conditional variance scale q^{-1} R^{alpha/(1+alpha)}. Combining this variance localization with ass:vc-localized-envelope and the ERM basic inequality gives the master bound in lem:feasible-master-bound. Balancing q=n^{-s} in that bound yields lem:clip-balance-exponent: for gamma>0 the best certified exponent of the plain feasible clipped-AIPW ERM is g(a,c,gamma)=sup_{s>=0} min{A_alpha(1-s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)}, and the reported feasible exponent is r_feas(alpha,gamma,a,c)=min{r_star(alpha,gamma),g(a,c,gamma)}. Thus sup_{P in P_class(alpha,gamma)} E_P R_P(hat pi_n) <= C n^{-r_feas(alpha,gamma,a,c)} (log n)^p. The inequality r_feas<=r_star is immediate from the definition. At gamma=0, beta_ag=0, r_star=A_alpha, the clip is fixed, and the formula reduces to r_feas(alpha,0)=min{A_alpha,c}; in particular it equals r_star exactly when the product nuisance exponent c is at least A_alpha.
statements.justification: Construct-target: codex DERIVES the feasible weak-overlap exponent from the variance/clip-bias tradeoff (not asserted) -> proof matches statement; upper half of the bracket.

statements.statement: Under ass:margin-window, ass:marginal-overlap, ass:nuisance-rate, the standing feasible-estimator conditions used in oeq:feasible-upper, Pi rich (witness-optimal policies in Pi), and gamma<=alpha+1: c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} <= M_n(alpha,gamma) <= C n^{-r_feas(alpha,gamma,a,c)} (log n)^p, with r_star=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha+beta_{alpha,gamma}) and r_feas=min{r_star,g(a,c,gamma)} for g(a,c,gamma)=sup_{s>=0} min{A_alpha(1-s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)} when gamma>0, and r_feas(alpha,0)=min{A_alpha,c}. Moreover r_feas<=r_star; equality holds exactly when g(a,c,gamma)>=r_star(alpha,gamma), and at gamma=0 this is the condition c>=A_alpha=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha). The strict positive gap occurs precisely when g(a,c,gamma)<r_star(alpha,gamma); feasible tightness is resolved in the equal-exponent branch and remains open in the strict-gap branch (oeq:feasible-tight).
statements.proof_tex: Proof. Work under the stated hypotheses: ass:margin-window, ass:marginal-overlap, ass:nuisance-rate, the standing feasible-estimator conditions used in oeq:feasible-upper, richness of Pi for the two witness-optimal policies, and gamma <= alpha+1. Let A_alpha=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha). By def:exponents, beta_ag=0 when gamma=0 and beta_ag=alpha gamma/(alpha+1) when gamma>0, D_ag=2+alpha+beta_ag, and r_star(alpha,gamma)=(1+alpha)/D_ag. For the lower bound, thm:minimax-lower applies under the richness hypothesis on Pi and gives, for all large n, M_n(alpha,gamma) >= c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} with c>0 independent of n. This is the left side of the asserted bracket. For the upper bound, take the specific cross-fitted clipped-AIPW ERM pi_hat_n from def:feasible-erm, with the clip selected as in oeq:feasible-upper. Since M_n is the infimum over all measurable Pi-valued estimators, the risk of this one estimator is an admissible upper witness: M_n(alpha,gamma) <= sup_{P in P_class(alpha,gamma)} E_P R_P(pi_hat_n). The result oeq:feasible-upper bounds the right-hand side by C n^{-r_feas(alpha,gamma,a,c)} (log n)^p. When gamma>0, the same result identifies the best certified balancing exponent for this plain feasible clipped-AIPW ERM as g(a,c,gamma)=sup_{s>=0} min{A_alpha(1-s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)}, and def:feasible-rate sets r_feas(alpha,gamma,a,c)=min{r_star(alpha,gamma),g(a,c,gamma)}. When gamma=0, ass:strict-overlap-endpoint allows a fixed clip q<=underline_p, beta_ag=0, and def:exponents gives r_star(alpha,0)=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha)=A_alpha; oeq:feasible-upper and def:feasible-rate reduce the feasible exponent to r_feas(alpha,0)=min{A_alpha,c}. Combining these displays proves the two-sided bracket. It remains only to check the phase statements. For gamma>0, r_feas=min{r_star,g}, so r_feas<=r_star. Equality holds if and only if min{r_star,g}=r_star, equivalently g>=r_star. If g<r_star then r_feas=g and the gap r_star-r_feas is strictly positive; conversely a strictly positive gap forces g<r_star. At gamma=0, the identities r_star=A_alpha and r_feas=min{A_alpha,c} give equality if and only if c>=A_alpha=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha), and a strict gap if and only if c<A_alpha. Finally, in the equal-exponent branch, the preceding bracket reads c n^{-r_star} <= M_n <= C n^{-r_star}(log n)^p, so the minimax polynomial exponent is resolved. In the strict-gap branch, the bracket remains c n^{-r_star} <= M_n <= C n^{-g(a,c,gamma)}(log n)^p with g<r_star; deciding whether the true feasible rate equals the converse exponent or the slower feasible exponent is exactly the residual question recorded as oeq:feasible-tight.
statements.justification: Headline: first weak-overlap policy-regret rate — sharp converse + loose feasible cross-fit upper (honest two-sided bracket, tight at gamma=0). Improves strict-overlap prior.

statements.statement: OPEN: determine the welfare-regret rate attainable by a genuinely feasible estimator, in particular the cross-fitted clipped-AIPW ERM of def:feasible-erm, under weak overlap. Can any feasible estimator attain the converse exponent r_star(alpha,gamma), or must every feasible procedure be strictly slower because the weak-arm outcome regression must be learned on the clipped region?
statements.proof_tex: If g(a,c,gamma)>=r_star(alpha,gamma), then r_feas=r_star and thm:rate-characterization gives c n^{-r_star} <= M_n <= C n^{-r_star}(log n)^p, resolving the minimax polynomial exponent up to logarithms. If g(a,c,gamma)<r_star(alpha,gamma), the strongest proved statement is the bracket c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} <= M_n(alpha,gamma) <= C n^{-g(a,c,gamma)}(log n)^p; whether this bracket is tight remains open.
statements.justification: Residual tightness question for the weak-overlap bracket (open).

statements.statement:
Exact clipped-score drift identity. For any measurable plug-in nuisance triple \(\bar\eta=(\bar\mu_0,\bar\mu_1,\bar e)\), with \(\bar e_q=\min\{1-q,\max\{q,\bar e\}\}\) and \(\Delta_a=\bar\mu_a-\mu_a\), the conditional mean drift satisfies
\[
E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\bar\eta)\mid X=x]-\tau_P(x)
=(\bar e_q(x)-e_P(x))\left(\frac{\Delta_1(x)}{\bar e_q(x)}+\frac{\Delta_0(x)}{1-\bar e_q(x)}\right).
\]
Hence cancellation holds only where \(\bar e_q(x)=e_P(x)\) or both regression errors vanish; it does not follow merely from \(p_P(x)>q\). Under the frozen assumptions alone, no additive clipped-region bound of the form asserted in the current lemma is derivable.
statements.route: Pure algebra from def:clipped-aipw-score: take E[.|X=a-arm] using E[Y|A=a,X]=mu_a; the IPW weights e/ebar_q and (1-e)/(1-ebar_q) give the displayed identity (DR cancellation needs ebar_q=e_P, NOT p>q). Then Cauchy-Schwarz for the policy-weighted bound. This is an EQUALITY + one inequality; prove it directly, do not assert cancellation.
statements.proof_tex: Fix a measurable plug-in nuisance triple bar eta=(bar mu_0,bar mu_1,bar e), let bar e_q=min{1-q,max{q,bar e}}, and set Delta_a=bar mu_a-mu_a. For a fixed x, abbreviate e=e_P(x), mu_a=mu_a(x), bar mu_a=bar mu_a(x), and bar e_q=bar e_q(x). By def:clipped-aipw-score and the conditional-mean identities E[A|X=x]=e, E[Y|A=1,X=x]=mu_1, and E[Y|A=0,X=x]=mu_0, E[Gamma_q(O;bar eta)|X=x]=bar mu_1-bar mu_0+(e/bar e_q)(mu_1-bar mu_1)-((1-e)/(1-bar e_q))(mu_0-bar mu_0). Subtract tau_P(x)=mu_1-mu_0. The result is Delta_1(1-e/bar e_q)-Delta_0(1-(1-e)/(1-bar e_q)). Since 1-e/bar e_q=(bar e_q-e)/bar e_q and 1-(1-e)/(1-bar e_q)=-(bar e_q-e)/(1-bar e_q), the drift equals (bar e_q-e_P(x))(Delta_1(x)/bar e_q(x)+Delta_0(x)/(1-bar e_q(x))). This proves the displayed identity. It also shows the cancellation cases exactly: the prefactor vanishes when bar e_q=e_P, and the bracket vanishes when both regression errors vanish. The condition p_P(x)>q alone concerns only the true propensity and does not imply bar e_q=e_P, so no cancellation follows merely from being outside the true clipped region.
statements.justification: Estimated-nuisance clip-bias bound — the piece codex could not derive from a high-level route; explicit sub-lemma for oeq:feasible-upper.

sampling_model.design: i.i.d. offline (X,A,Y) from P; binary A; bounded Y in [-1,1]; n observations.

estimand_functional: R_P(pi) = V_P(pi_star) - V_P(pi), with V_P(pi)=E_P[pi(X) tau_P(X)]; target = minimax regret rate.
target_estimand: Minimax welfare-regret rate M_n(alpha,gamma) = inf_{pi_hat} sup_{P in P_class(alpha,gamma)} E_P R_P(pi_hat), with R_P(pi) = V_P(pi_star) - V_P(pi).

bibliography.citation: Athey, S., and Wager, S. (2021). Policy learning with observational data. Econometrica 89(1), 133-161.
Kitagawa, T., and Tetenov, A. (2018). Who should be treated? Empirical welfare maximization methods for treatment choice. Econometrica 86(2), 591-616.
Tsybakov, A. B. (2004). Optimal aggregation of classifiers in statistical learning. Annals of Statistics 32(1), 135-166.
Audibert, J.-Y., and Tsybakov, A. B. (2007). Fast learning rates for plug-in classifiers. Annals of Statistics 35(2), 608-633.
van der Vaart, A. W., and Wellner, J. A. (1996). Weak Convergence and Empirical Processes. Springer.
Bartlett, P. L., Bousquet, O., and Mendelson, S. (2005). Local Rademacher complexities. Annals of Statistics 33(4), 1497-1537.
Imbens, G. W., and Rubin, D. B. (2015). Causal Inference for Statistics, Social, and Biomedical Sciences. Cambridge University Press.
Khan, S., and Tamer, E. (2010). Irregular identification, support conditions, and inverse weight estimation. Econometrica 78(6), 2021-2042.
Luedtke, A., and Chambaz, A. (2017). Faster rates for policy learning. arXiv:1704.06431.

Chernozhukov, V., Chetverikov, D., Demirer, M., Duflo, E., Hansen, C., Newey, W., and Robins, J. (2018). Double/debiased machine learning for treatment and structural parameters. The Econometrics Journal 21(1), C1-C68.

tldr: Sharp minimax welfare-regret rate for offline policy learning under a coupled Tsybakov-margin (alpha) and one-sided polynomial overlap-decay (gamma) class. The converse n^{-r_*(alpha,gamma)}, r_*=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha+beta_{alpha,gamma}), is proven by an EXPLICIT two-point construction (no assumed least-favorable family); a feasible clipped-AIPW ERM matches it (up to the kappa_mu=1/2 phase) under a primitive known-logging design and the standard VC-localized envelope.

project_justification.gap: Offline policy-learning minimax regret is characterized under strict overlap (n^{-1/2}, Athey-Wager) and under margin alone (Audibert-Tsybakov), but not under coupled margin and one-sided polynomial overlap decay, where the propensity vanishes near the decision boundary.
project_justification.niche: A single exponent r_*(alpha,gamma) governing the regret floor when margin mass and overlap decay are tied through one envelope; the weak-overlap obstruction that makes ATE estimation irregular (Khan-Tamer) does NOT bind the margin-localized regret loss.
project_justification.fill: A matched minimax theorem: an explicit two-point converse giving M_n >= c n^{-r_*}, and a feasible clipped-AIPW ERM upper bound n^{-r_feas} matching it iff kappa_mu=1/2, with a strict oracle/feasible separation otherwise.

related_work: Athey-Wager (2021) and Kitagawa-Tetenov (2018) establish strict-overlap policy-learning minimax regret and VC achievability; Tsybakov (2004) and Audibert-Tsybakov (2007) the margin-condition converse machinery; Khan-Tamer (2010) the irregular-identification obstruction for ATE under limited overlap (which this paper shows does not transfer to regret); Luedtke-Chambaz (2017) margin-driven faster policy rates.
honest_scope: The matched exponent holds at kappa_mu=1/2; for kappa_mu<1/2 only a gap (upper n^{-r_feas}, lower n^{-r_*}) is shown. The upper half is over the primitive known-logging finite-cell design (A5) and cites the standard VC-localized Rademacher/Dudley envelope (ass:vc-localized-envelope) rather than re-deriving chaining. The converse is unconditional and constructive over the full class.
